title: Nanjamma vs Muniyamma on 9 March, 2026

Advocate for D1, D5) (D2(a) to (e) - Exparte) (D3(a)( to (c)- Exparte) (D4(a) to (c) - Exparte) (Sri D.Manjunath, Advocate for Defendants No.6 to 9, 13 and 14) (Sri M.Shivaprakash, Advocate for-Lrs of D9 and D10, 11 and 12) (Sri G.S.Prasad, Advocate for D15) (D17- Exparte) (Sri B.T.Prasanna Kumar, Advocate for D16, 18) (D19 to 21 - Exparte) (Sri N.Srinivas, Advocate for D22) (D23, 24 - Exparte) (Sri B.L.Ranganath, Advocate for D25) (D26, 27, 28- Exparte) (Sri A.M.Suresh, Advocate for D29-33) (D34-Exparte) Date of institution of the suit: 02.01.2012 Nature of the suit: Suit for partition 9 O.S.69/2012 Judgment Date of commencement of 15.07.2022 recording of evidence: Date on which the judgment 09.03.2026 was pronounced; Total duration: Years Months Days 14 02 10 JUDGMENT This suit is filed by the plaintiffs seeking partition and separate possession of 4/9th share in the suit schedule properties and for consequential relief of declaration and permanent injunction. 2. The brief facts of the case of the plaintiff is that the one Muninanjappa was the Mulapurusha of the family of the plaintiffs and defendants No.1 to 5. The said Muninanjappa had two daughters namely Ammayamma and Chikka Thayamma and one son by name Thimmaiah. The said daughters died issueless. The plaintiffs and defendants No.2 to 5 are the children of the said Thimmaiah, and defendant No.1 is the wife of the said Thimmaiah. During the lifetime of Thimmaiah, the plaintiffs and defendants No.1 to 5 constituted a Hindu 10 O.S.69/2012 Judgment Joint Family. 3. The plaintiffs submit that land bearing Sy.No.15/1 measuring 1 acre 9 guntas, land bearing Sy.No.292/1A measuring 37 guntas, and land bearing Sy.No.85/1 measuring 2 acres 31 guntas, all situated at Devarachikkanahalli Village, Begur Hobli, Bangalore South Taluk, more fully described in the schedule hereunder and hereinafter referred to as the suit schedule properties, are the ancestral and joint family properties of the plaintiffs and defendants No.1 to 5. 4. The plaintiffs submit that they and defendants No.1 to 5 are members of the joint family and that the suit schedule properties are joint family properties. They have been in joint possession and enjoyment of the same. 5. The plaintiffs submit that they are entitled to 4/9th share together in the suit schedule properties. It is further submitted that defendants No.1 to 5 have started acting detrimental to the interest of the plaintiffs in respect of the schedule properties and have begun secreting 11 O.S.69/2012 Judgment matters pertaining thereto, thereby compelling the plaintiffs to demand partition by metes and bounds and allotment of their legitimate 4/9th share. 6. The plaintiffs submit that defendants No.1 to 5, on one pretext or another, have been evading partition of the suit schedule properties. The plaintiffs, having developed suspicion over their conduct, verified the revenue records and, to their shock and surprise, in the second week of February 2011 came to know that their father late Thimmaiah and defendants No.1 to 5 had executed a sale deed dated 28-09-1981, and that defendants No.6 to 12 had in turn alienated the suit schedule properties in favour of defendants No.13 and 14 under a sale deed dated 11-05-2011. 7. The plaintiffs submit that defendants No.1 to 5 have created certain fictitious documents in favour of defendants No.15 to 34 in respect of Item No.2 of the suit schedule properties. 8. The plaintiffs further submit that defendants 12 O.S.69/2012 Judgment No.1 to 5 and their father late Thimmaiah created certain fictitious documents in favour of one Pilla Reddy, the husband of defendant No.30 and father of defendants No.31 to 34, in respect of Item No.3 of the suit schedule properties. 9. The plaintiffs submit that defendants No.1 to 5 and late Thimmaiah had no exclusive right, title or possession over the suit schedule properties and therefore had no authority to alienate the same or any portion thereof. 10. The plaintiffs submit that the aforesaid sale deeds are fictitious and fabricated behind their back and that they are not parties to the alleged transactions. The said sale transactions are not binding on the plaintiffs' 4/9th share in the suit schedule properties. Defendants No.6 to 34 have not derived any right, title or possession under the alleged sale transactions. 11. The plaintiffs submit that notwithstanding the alleged sale transactions, they and defendants No.1 to 5 13 O.S.69/2012 Judgment have continued in joint possession and enjoyment of the suit schedule properties and that they are entitled to 4/9th share therein. 12. The plaintiffs submit that defendants No.1 to 5 have refused to effect partition by metes and bounds in the second week of February 2011, necessitating the present suit. 13. The plaintiffs submit that defendants No.6 to 14, taking undue advantage of the sale deed in their favour, though having no valid right, title or possession, are contemplating putting up unauthorized construction in Item No.1 of the suit schedule properties. 14. The plaintiffs submit that defendants No.15 to 29, having no right, title or possession over Item No.2 of the suit schedule properties, are contemplating construction on the basis of fictitious documents allegedly executed by defendants No.2 to 5, with mala-fide intention to change the nature of the property. 15. Similarly, defendants No.30 to 34 are 14 O.S.69/2012 Judgment contemplating putting up structures on Item No.3 of the suit schedule properties under the guise of an alleged sale deed executed by late Thimmaiah. 16. The plaintiffs submit that defendants No.6 to 34 have no right, title or possession over the suit schedule properties and are not entitled to put up any structures thereon. Hence, it is just and necessary to restrain them by way of permanent injunction. 17. The plaintiffs further submit that defendants No.6 to 34, taking undue advantage of the alleged sale deeds and with mala-fide intention to defraud the plaintiffs of their legitimate share, are attempting to alienate or create encumbrances over the suit schedule properties in favour of third parties. Hence, they are liable to be restrained by permanent injunction. Hence, the plaintiffs pray this Court to pass judgment and decree (a) Direct the defendants to effect partition of the suit schedule properties by metes and bounds and allot 4/9th share together to the plaintiffs; (b) In default, effect partition 15 O.S.69/2012 Judgment through Court; (c) Declare that the sale deeds dated 28-09-1981, 11-05-2011 and 04-04-1968, insofar as they relate to the plaintiffs' 4/9th share, are not binding on them; (d) Restrain defendants No.6 to 34 from putting up any structures on the suit schedule properties by permanent injunction; (e) Restrain defendants No.6 to 34 from alienating or creating encumbrances over the suit schedule properties by permanent injunction; (f) Grant such other reliefs including costs as deemed fit in the interest of justice and equity. SUIT SCHEDULE PROPERTIES Item No.1: All that piece and parcel of the property bearing Sy. No.15/1, measuring 1 acre 9 guntas, situated at Devarachikkanahalli, Begur Hobli, Bangalore South Taluk. East : Government Road West : Shanbhog Land North : Private Property South : 80 Feet Road Item No.2: All that piece and parcel of the property bearing Sy. No.292/1A, measuring 37 guntas, situated at 16 O.S.69/2012 Judgment Devarachikkanahalli, Begur Hobli, Bangalore South Taluk bounded on. East : Krishna Reddy's Land West : Pilla Reddy's Land North: Reddappa and Appanna Reddy's Land South: Narayana Reddy and Krishna Reddy's Land Item No.3: All that piece and parcel of the property bearing Sy. No.85/1, measuring 2 acres 31 guntas, situated at Devarachikkanahalli, Begur Hobli, Bangalore South Taluk bounded on:- East : Sadashivappa's Land West : Narappa's Land North : Venkataramanappa's Land South : Road. 18. In response to the suit summons the defendants appeared through their respective counsels and the Defendants No.9 to 12 filed their written statement in detail contending that the suit instituted by the plaintiffs is manifestly frivolous, vexatious and wholly untenable in law, on facts and on probabilities. These defendants have purchased the property bearing Sy. No.15/1 measuring 1 17 O.S.69/2012 Judgment Acre 9 Guntas under a registered Sale Deed dated 28-09- 1981 for valuable consideration from their vendors, namely defendants No.1 to 8, who were the absolute owners possessing alienable and marketable title. Title and possession were lawfully conveyed in favour of P. Narayana Navada. The plaintiffs, after a lapse of more than three decades, have now filed the present suit seeking partition and claiming 4/9th share, which claim is hopelessly barred by limitation and is not maintainable in view of the registered instrument of sale. Under these circumstances, the suit is liable to be dismissed with exemplary costs under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure. 19. The suit is collusive in nature. Defendants No.1 to 5 and defendants No.13 and 14 have, with oblique motive and for extraneous considerations, colluded to institute this frivolous litigation. The intention appears to be to compel and coerce these defendants to part with property in their lawful possession and enjoyment. On this ground alone, the suit deserves dismissal. 18 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 20. The suit is further not maintainable on the ground of improper valuation and insufficient court fee. The sale transaction dates back to the year 1981. The property is situated within the limits of the Bangalore Mahanagara Palike and constitutes urban land, not agricultural land. Therefore, valuation must be based on market value and appropriate court fee must be paid. 21. The suit is also bad for non-joinder of necessary parties. The branches of late Muninanjappa and late Thimmaiah have not been properly arrayed as parties. On this ground as well, the suit is not maintainable. 22. With reference to paragraph 3 of the plaint, these defendants submit that the alleged inter-relationship and genealogical tree produced as Document No.1 are not within their knowledge and are put to strict proof. The assertion that defendants No.1 to 5 constituted a Hindu Joint Family is specifically denied and is put to strict proof. The further allegation that Sy. No.15/1, Sy. No.292/1A and Sy. No.85/1 of Devarachikkanahalli are ancestral and 19 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment joint family properties of the plaintiffs and defendants No.1 to 5 is specifically denied. These defendants confine their defence strictly to Sy. No.15/1. They have no interest in the remaining lands, which are not germane to the present adjudication. The inclusion of other items appears deliberate, intended to mislead and confuse the issues. 23. With reference to paragraph 5 of the plaint, the statement that plaintiffs and defendants No.1 to 5 were members of a joint family and that the properties were joint family properties in joint possession and enjoyment is categorically denied. The claim of entitlement to 4/9th share is also denied and is put to strict proof. Item No.1 of the schedule property has, since 1981, neither constituted joint family property nor was it ever in joint possession of the plaintiffs or defendants No.1 to 5. 24. With reference to paragraph 7 of the plaint, the alleged demand for partition does not arise. The property ceased to belong to the family of Thimmaiah in the year 1981 upon execution of a registered Sale Deed for valuable 20 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment consideration. The statements made by the plaintiffs are mischievous and factually erroneous. The claim for partition is legally unsustainable. These defendants, in exercise of lawful ownership rights, alienated a portion of the property in the year 2011 to meet family and legal necessities. There was no concealment in acquisition of title or exercise of ownership rights. 25. With reference to paragraph 9 of the plaint, the allegation regarding creation of fictitious documents in favour of one Pilla Reddy, husband of defendant No.30, is not within the knowledge of these defendants and is not germane to Item No.1 purchased absolutely in the year 1981. 26. With reference to paragraph 11 of the plaint, the allegation that the sale deeds are fictitious and fabricated is baseless. The Sale Deed of 1981 is a duly registered instrument executed for valuable consideration. Having alienated the property thereunder, the plaintiffs' allegations are mischievous and intended to prejudice this Court. 21 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment These defendants have acquired unimpeachable title and exercised rights of ownership lawfully. The present claim, after three decades, is a motivated and extraneous attempt. 27. The averments in the plaint in respect of Item No.1 lack substance and truth. Defendants No.13 and 14 are developers engaged in commercial activity and have developed a portion of the property for profit. The remaining portion in possession of these defendants appears to be an eyesore to defendants No.13 and 14. At their instance, this litigation has been instituted with an oblique motive to coerce these defendants into parting with their remaining property. The suit is therefore frivolous, vexatious and not maintainable. 28. There is no real cause of action. The alleged cause of action is artificial and created solely for instituting this suit. 29. It is further contended that In the year 1981, the Moolapurusha, namely Muniveerappa @ Munigangappa, and his children sold Sy. No.15/1 of 22 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment Devarachikkanahalli measuring 1 Acre 9 Guntas under a registered Sale Deed bearing No.3612 dated 13-10-2008 registered before the Sub-Registrar. Since then, these defendants have been in continuous, absolute possession and enjoyment of the property. Subsequently, a Notification was issued by the Bangalore Development Authority for acquisition for formation of BTM 4th Stage layout. The same was challenged before the Hon'ble High Court of Karnataka. There was also family litigation in O.S. No.8285/1996 for partition and separate possession, which has been decreed and final decree drawn. 30. The plaintiffs never constituted a joint family in respect of the suit property, nor was the property ancestral at any point of time. The suit, filed without challenging the registered instrument and without possession, is not maintainable and is liable to be dismissed. Hence, Defendants No.9 to 12 pray that this Court to dismiss the suit with exemplary costs in the interest of justice and equity. 23 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 31. The defendants No.13 and 14 appeared through their counsel and filed their written statement contending that the suit, insofar as it relates to 0-26 guntas comprised in Item No.1 of the plaint schedule as against defendants No.13 and 14, is wholly not maintainable either in law or on facts and is liable to be dismissed in limine. 32. With reference to paragraph 3 of the plaint, these defendants do not admit that one Muninanjappa was the Moolapurusha of the family of the plaintiffs, nor that the plaintiffs are the daughters of Thimmaiah. The plaintiffs are put to strict proof of their alleged relationship with Muninanjappa and Thimmaiah. It is categorically denied that during the lifetime of Thimmaiah, the plaintiffs constituted a joint family along with defendants No.1 to 5. 33. With reference to paragraphs 4 and 5 of the plaint, it is emphatically denied that Item No.1 of the schedule property was ancestral or joint family property of the plaintiffs. It is further categorically denied that the 24 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment plaintiffs are in joint possession and enjoyment of Item No.1 of the plaint schedule. 34. With reference to paragraphs 6, 7 and 10 of the plaint, the averments that the plaintiffs together are entitled to 4/9th share in Item No.1 of the plaint schedule and that defendants No.1 to 5 are acting detrimental to their interest are specifically denied as false. The further contention that defendants No.1 to 5 evaded partition by metes and bounds and that the plaintiffs came to know of the sale deeds dated 28-09-1981 and 11-05-2011 only in the second week of February 2011 are false and invented for the purpose of the suit. It is categorically denied that defendants No.1 to 5 and late Thimmaiah had no exclusive right, title or possession over Item No.1 or that they lacked authority to alienate the same. 35. With reference to paragraphs 11, 12 and 13 of the plaint, it is categorically denied that the sale deeds pertaining to Item No.1 are fictitious or fabricated or not binding on the plaintiffs. It is specifically denied that 25 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment defendants No.6 to 14 did not derive valid right, title and possession in respect of Item No.1. It is further denied that the plaintiffs are in joint possession of Item No.1 or entitled to 4/9th share therein. The allegation that defendants No.1 to 5 refused partition in February 2011 is based on mere assumptions and is denied. 36. With reference to paragraphs 14, 17, 18 and 19 of the plaint, it is categorically denied that defendants No.13 and 14 are attempting to put up unauthorized construction in Item No.1. It is denied that defendants No.6 to 14 lack right, title or possession in respect of Item No.1. It is emphatically denied that the plaintiffs are entitled to permanent injunction restraining defendants No.6 to 14 from construction or alienation. The plaintiffs have no right, title or interest, much less joint possession, over Item No.1. The allegation that defendants are attempting to defraud the plaintiffs is imaginary and untenable. The alleged date of knowledge in February 2011 is falsely pleaded to circumvent the bar of limitation. 26 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 37. With reference to paragraphs 20 and 21 of the plaint, it is submitted that there is no cause of action for the suit and the alleged date of accrual is fictitious. The plaintiffs have wrongly valued the suit under Section 35(ii) of the Karnataka Court Fees and Suits Valuation Act on the false premise of joint possession over Item No.1. 38. These defendants submit that they are the lawful owners in actual physical possession of 0-26 guntas of converted land forming part of Item No.1 of the plaint schedule. The said converted portion of 0-26 guntas is hereinafter referred to as the "Written Statement Schedule Property". 39. It is submitted that one Muniveerappa @ Munigangappa had four sons, namely Muninanjappa, Nagappa, Munishamappa and Chinnappa. During his lifetime, Muniveerappa along with his sons and grandsons constituted a joint family which owned and possessed Sy. No.15/1 measuring 1 acre 9 guntas including 2 guntas kharab as ancestral and joint family property. After his 27 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment demise, the four sons and their lineal descendants continued in joint possession as co-parceners. One of the grandsons of Thimmaiah was shown as cultivator in Column No.12(2) of the RTC. 40. In the year 1981, on 28-09-1981, the surviving co-parceners representing each branch executed a registered Sale Deed in favour of defendants No.6 and 9 jointly in respect of Sy. No.15/1. The said document was registered as No.3612, pages 167-173, Vol.1732, Book I, dated 13-10-1981 before the Sub-Registrar, Bangalore North Taluk. Consequently, defendants No.6 and 9 acquired absolute ownership and possession over the entire extent of 1 acre 9 guntas, and their names were mutated in the revenue records as khatedars and cultivators vide M.R. No.4/81-82. 41. In 1988, the Bangalore Development Authority initiated acquisition proceedings for formation of BTM 4th Stage Layout and issued preliminary notification dated 15- 12-1988 and final notification dated 03-11-1990 vide 28 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment No.HUD/334/MNX/90 under Section 19(1) of the Bangalore Development Authority Act, 1976, covering the entire extent of Sy. No.15/1. 42. In 1996, defendant No.6 instituted O.S. No.8285/1996 for partition and separate possession in respect of Item No.1 before the City Civil Court, Bengaluru. As per the final decree dated 29-11-1996, subsequently registered as No.BAS-1-05401-2005-06 dated 05-07-2005 before the Sub-Registrar, Bangalore South Taluk, the property was divided equally between defendants No.6 and 9, each receiving 0-24½ guntas. 43. During 2010, the Government of Karnataka, Urban Development Department, vide Order dated 25-08- 2010 No.UDD/397/MNX-010, de-notified 0-26 guntas in Sy. No.15/1 from acquisition. Thereafter, defendants No.6 and 9 jointly applied for conversion of 0-26 guntas for non- agricultural residential purpose. Upon payment of Rs.35,448/- towards conversion and phodi charges under challan dated 01-01-2011 and issuance of Official 29 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment Memorandum dated 06-01-2011 No.ALN(SB)SR-103/2010- 11 by the Special Deputy Commissioner, Bangalore District, conversion was granted. The property was demarcated in survey sketch dated 20-09-2010, assessed to tax, and taxes paid. Betterment charges of Rs.10,52,565/- were paid to BBMP vide receipt dated 11- 02-2011. BBMP issued Khata Certificate No.720/15/1 dated 17-02-2011 jointly in favour of defendants No.6 and 9. Of the 0-26 guntas, 0-18 guntas belonged to defendant No.6 and 0-8 guntas to defendant No.9. 44. Thereafter, defendants No.6 to 12 executed a registered Sale Deed dated 11-05-2011 in favour of defendants No.13 and 14 in respect of the Written Statement Schedule Property. Consequently, these defendants became absolute owners in possession and enjoyment. BBMP mutated khata in their favour. They have paid property taxes, obtained licence and sanctioned plan dated 18-10-2011 for construction of residential apartment comprising BF+GF+4 Upper Floors, and have commenced construction after investing substantial 30 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment amounts. 45. Subject to proof of plaintiffs' relationship with late Thimmaiah, it is submitted that the plaintiffs were not co-parceners as on the date of Sale Deed dated 28-09-1981 and therefore have no locus standi to seek partition or challenge the sale deeds. The suit is barred by limitation. By virtue of the proviso to Section 6(1) of the Hindu Succession (Amendment) Act, 2005 (Act No.39 of 2005), any disposition or alienation prior to 20-12-2004 remains unaffected. Hence, the Sale Deed of 1981 in favour of defendants No.6 and 9 and the subsequent Sale Deed of 2011 in favour of defendants No.13 and 14 are valid and binding. The suit is liable to be dismissed at the threshold. 46. Though fully aware of these factual and legal aspects, the plaintiffs have instituted this false and frivolous suit with ulterior motive to extract money from defendants No.13 and 14. The suit deserves dismissal in limine with costs. 47. The averments relating to Items No.2 and 3 of 31 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment the plaint schedule do not concern defendants No.13 and 14. The plaintiffs are called upon to prove their correct age, as incorrect age has been shown in the cause title. All contentions not specifically admitted herein are denied. Hence, defendants No.13 and 14 pray to dismiss the suit in respect of the Written Statement Schedule Property with exemplary costs. Defendants No.13 and 14's written statement schedule property All that piece and parcel of converted land measuring 0-26 guntas in Sy. No.15/1, situated at Devarachikkanahalli, Begur Hobli, Bangalore South Taluk, now within BBMP limits, bearing Khata No.720/15/1 (measuring 27,537 sq. ft.), bounded by: East: Road West: Private Property North: Remaining extent of Sy. No.15/1 belonging to P. Narayana Navada South: 80 Feet Road. 48. In response to the suit summons the defendant No.15 appeared through his counsel and filed his written statement contending that the suit of the Plaintiffs is not maintainable either in law or on facts, and hence the same 32 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment is liable to be dismissed in limine. 49. This Defendant submits that, it is true that one Muni Nanjappa was the Mulapurusha of the family of the Plaintiffs and Defendant Nos.1 to 5. It is submitted that, the said Muninanjappa had two daughters by name Ammayamma and Chikka Thayamma and a son by Name Thimmaiah. The said two daughters of Muninanjappa died issue-less. It is submitted that the Plaintiffs and Defendant Nos.2 to 5 are the children of said Thimmaiah and the 1st Defendant is the wife of said Thimmaiah is true. Further it is true that, during the life time of said Thimmaiah, the Plaintiffs and Defendant Nos. 1 to 5 have constituted a Hindu Joint Family. 50. This Defendant submit that, it is true that the land bearing Sy.No.15/1, measuring an extent 1 acre 9 guntas and land bearing Sy.No.292/1A, measuring an extent 37 guntas and Land Sy.No.85/1, measuring an extent 2 acres 31 guntas, all are situated at Devarachikkanahalli Village, Begur Hobli, Bangalore South 33 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment Taluk, which are morefully described hereunder and referred to as the schedule properties, it is not true say that the suit schedule properties are their ancestral and joint family properties of the Plaintiffs and Defendant Nos. 1 to 5. Since, the suit schedule properties were sold during the lifetime of the father of Plaintiffs and Defendant Nos.2 to 5 including their mother, the Defendant No.1 in the year 1981. 51. This Defendant submit that, the Plaintiffs and the Defendant No.1 to 5 are the members of joint family and the schedule properties are the joint family properties of the Plaintiffs and Defendant Nos. 1 to 5 and they have been in joint possession and enjoyment of the suit schedule properties are all false. The said properties were ancestral Joint Family Properties prior to 1981, thereafter the said suit schedule properties were alienated by the father of Plaintiffs and Defendant Nos.2 to 5 including their mother, the Defendant No.1 in favour of third parties. Subsequent to their purchase, they developed the layout and they sold the land in the form of sites to the 34 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment subsequent purchasers. After purchase of such sites they constructed houses in the suit schedule properties as on 1983 to 1985. Now, the land described in the suit schedule are not available since the nature of the suit schedule properties were changed into residential layout and houses, roads and drainages were constructed in the suit schedule properties. For kind perusal of this Hon'ble Court, this Defendant has produced the satellite images for suit schedule properties for the year 2008, 2010, 2012, 2015 and 2024 which clearly shows there is no land available as stated in the suit schedule properties. Since, the suit schedule properties were changed into revenue layout in the year 1983. Issues No.1 and 2: Both these issues are interconnected and go to the root of the plaintiffs' claim for partition and separate possession. The facts require for the discussion of other are same. The finding on one issue will have a great bearing on the other. Hence, these issues are taken together for common discussion. 98. The burden squarely lies upon the plaintiffs to establish that they and Defendant Nos.1 to 5 constitute members of a Hindu Undivided Joint Family and that the suit schedule properties form part of the joint family estate. Unless the plaintiffs first establish the existence of a joint family nucleus and the joint family character of the property, the relief of partition cannot be granted. 99. In the plaint, the plaintiffs have asserted that they and Defendant Nos.1 to 5 are descendants of a common ancestor and that the family possessed ancestral properties including the land bearing Sy.No.85/1 measuring 3 acres 4 guntas. It is their contention that the 54 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment said property continued as joint family property and that they are entitled to claim their respective shares therein. The plaintiffs further contend that the alienation made in the year 1968 is not binding on their alleged share and that the property continued to remain part of the joint family estate. 100. On the other hand, the contesting defendants in their written statements have categorically denied the existence of any subsisting joint family in respect of the suit schedule property. It is their specific contention that the land measuring 1 acre 22 guntas out of Sy.No.85/1 had been validly alienated under the registered Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 by competent members of the family including Thimmareddy, Venkataswamy Reddy and others, and that upon such alienation the property ceased to be part of the joint family estate. The defendants further contend that ever since the said sale, the purchasers and their successors have been in open, continuous and exclusive possession. 55 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 101. In order to substantiate their claim, the plaintiffs examined Plaintiff No.1 as PW1. In his chief- examination, PW1 reiterated the averments made in the plaint and deposed that the suit schedule property originally belonged to their ancestors and that the plaintiffs and Defendant Nos.1 to 5 constitute members of a Hindu joint family. However, during the course of cross- examination, PW1 admitted that he does not possess any documentary evidence showing that the property continued to stand in the name of the alleged joint family after the year 1968. 102. Furthermore, PW1 in his cross-examination admitted that the revenue records from the year 1972-73 onwards reflect the names of the purchasers and their successors in respect of the alienated extent of land. He also conceded that he had not produced any RTC extracts, mutation entries, tax receipts, or other documentary material to demonstrate that the plaintiffs or Defendant Nos.1 to 5 were in joint possession or enjoyment of the said property after the alleged alienation. These admissions 56 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment materially weaken the plaintiffs' claim of continued joint family ownership. 103. PW1 was also confronted with the registered Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968. Though he attempted to dispute its binding nature, he admitted during cross- examination that the executants of the sale deed included members of their family. He further admitted that neither he nor his predecessors had challenged the said sale for several decades. Such admissions assume significance as they indicate that the alienation was recognized and acted upon for a considerable length of time. 104. On the contrary, the defendants examined DW1 who supported the defence set out in the written statements. DW1 deposed that the property measuring 1 acre 22 guntas in Sy.No.85/1 had been lawfully purchased under the registered Sale Deed of 1968 and that ever since then the purchasers and their successors have been in continuous possession and enjoyment of the property. 57 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment DW1 also produced revenue records, mutation extracts, and tax paid receipts evidencing their possession. 105. During cross-examination, nothing substantial was elicited from DW1 to discredit his testimony. Though suggestions were made disputing the validity of the sale deed and the nature of possession, DW1 consistently maintained that the property had been purchased under a valid sale and that the defendants and their predecessors have been exercising acts of ownership for decades, including construction of buildings and payment of taxes. 106. It is a settled principle of Hindu law that the mere existence of a relationship among family members does not automatically establish the existence of joint family property. The party asserting that a particular property is joint family property must prove either that it was ancestral property or that it was acquired from the income of a joint family nucleus. In the absence of such proof, the presumption of joint family property cannot be drawn. 58 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 107. In the present case, the plaintiffs have failed to produce any reliable documentary evidence demonstrating that the suit schedule property continued to be part of the joint family estate after the execution of the Sale Deed in 1968. On the contrary, the documentary evidence produced by the defendants clearly indicates that the property stood transferred and that subsequent revenue records consistently recognize the rights of the purchasers. 108. The long lapse of time between the execution of the sale deed in 1968 and the institution of the present suit also assumes relevance. If the property had indeed continued as joint family property, the plaintiffs or their predecessors would have asserted their rights earlier. The prolonged silence and absence of any challenge to the alienation significantly undermine the credibility of the plaintiffs' claim. 109. Thus, the oral testimony of PW1, when read in conjunction with his admissions in cross-examination, fails to establish the existence of a subsisting Hindu undivided 59 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment joint family in respect of the suit schedule property. Conversely, the evidence of DW1 coupled with the documentary records lends support to the defence that the property had been validly alienated and ceased to be joint family property. 110. In view of the above discussion and appreciation of the oral and documentary evidence on record, this Court is of the considered opinion that the plaintiffs have failed to prove that they and Defendant Nos.1 to 5 constitute members of a Hindu undivided joint family in respect of the suit schedule property or that the suit schedule properties are the joint family properties of the plaintiffs and Defendant Nos.1 to 5. Accordingly, Issue Nos.1 and 2 are answered in the Negative. 111. Issue No.3:- The burden of proving joint possession and enjoyment of the suit schedule properties squarely lies upon the plaintiffs. In a suit for partition, the assertion of joint possession is a material averment, as the nature of possession determines not only the entitlement to 60 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment relief but also the valuation of the suit and the court fee payable. Therefore, the Court must carefully scrutinize the pleadings and evidence to ascertain whether the plaintiffs have established that they continued to be in joint possession along with the defendants. 112. In the plaint, the plaintiffs have contended that the suit schedule property forms part of the joint family estate and that the plaintiffs and Defendant Nos.1 to 5 are in joint possession and enjoyment thereof. It is further alleged that the defendants attempted to interfere with their rights, thereby necessitating the institution of the present suit seeking partition and separate possession of their alleged share. 113. The contesting defendants, however, have specifically denied this assertion in their written statements. They have contended that the plaintiffs have never been in possession of the suit schedule property and that the land measuring 1 acre 22 guntas out of Sy.No.85/1 had been alienated under the registered Sale 61 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment Deed dated 04.04.1968. According to the defendants, ever since the said alienation, the purchasers and their successors have been in exclusive possession and enjoyment of the property, and the plaintiffs have no manner of right, title, or possession over the same. 114. To substantiate their case, the plaintiffs examined Plaintiff No.1 as PW1. In his chief-examination affidavit, PW1 reiterated the averments made in the plaint and deposed that the plaintiffs and Defendant Nos.1 to 5 are in joint possession and enjoyment of the suit schedule property. However, such assertion in chief-examination requires corroboration through documentary evidence or other convincing material. 115. During the course of cross-examination, PW1 admitted that he had not produced any revenue records such as RTC extracts, mutation entries, or tax paid receipts standing in the names of the plaintiffs or Defendant Nos.1 to 5 to establish their joint possession. He also admitted that he had not cultivated the land nor 62 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment derived any income from the suit schedule property. These admissions materially weaken the claim of joint possession. 116. PW1 further admitted in cross-examination that the revenue records for several decades stand in the name of the purchasers and their successors. He also conceded that constructions have been put up on the property and that the defendants and their predecessors have been paying property taxes and exercising acts of ownership. Such admissions clearly indicate that the plaintiffs were not in possession or enjoyment of the suit schedule property. 117. On the other hand, the defendants examined DW1 who supported the defence set out in the written statements. DW1 deposed that the property was purchased under the registered Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 and that ever since the purchase, the defendants and their predecessors have been in continuous, open, and exclusive possession. DW1 also stated that buildings have been 63 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment constructed on the property and that municipal records and tax receipts stand in their names. 118. During cross-examination, DW1 consistently maintained that the plaintiffs were never in possession of the property and that the defendants alone have been exercising acts of ownership. Although suggestions were made disputing their possession, nothing material was elicited to discredit the testimony of DW1. His evidence remains substantially unshaken. 119. The documentary evidence produced by the defendants, including revenue records, mutation entries, and tax paid receipts, lends considerable support to the testimony of DW1. These records consistently reflect the names of the purchasers and their successors in possession of the property. Such long-standing entries in public records carry considerable evidentiary value and cannot be lightly ignored. 120. It is a settled principle that joint possession cannot be presumed in the face of clear evidence of 64 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment exclusive possession by one party. While co-parceners may ordinarily be presumed to be in constructive possession of joint family property, such presumption stands rebutted when documentary evidence and acts of ownership establish that one party has been in exclusive possession for a long period. 121. In the present case, the plaintiffs have failed to produce any material demonstrating their participation in the enjoyment of the property. There is no evidence of cultivation, collection of rent, payment of taxes, or exercise of any proprietary rights by them. The mere assertion of joint possession in the plaint and chief-examination of PW1, unsupported by documentary evidence, cannot outweigh the consistent records showing exclusive possession of the defendants. 122. The admissions made by PW1 during cross- examination, coupled with the documentary evidence and the testimony of DW1, clearly indicate that the defendants have been in exclusive possession and enjoyment of the 65 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment suit schedule property for several decades. Such evidence effectively rebuts the plaintiffs' claim of joint possession. 123. In view of the foregoing discussion and appreciation of the oral and documentary evidence on record, this Court is of the considered opinion that the plaintiffs have failed to prove that they and the defendants are in joint possession and enjoyment of the suit schedule properties. Accordingly, Issue No.3 is answered in the Negative. 124. Issue No.4:- The burden of proving this issue squarely rests upon the plaintiffs. The plaintiffs have specifically contended in the plaint that the suit schedule property was ancestral joint family property and that their father, Thimmaiah, along with Defendant Nos.1 to 5, had no exclusive right or authority to alienate the property. According to the plaintiffs, the alienation made under the registered Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 is not binding upon their alleged share. Therefore, it becomes necessary to examine whether the plaintiffs have established the 66 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment absence of legal authority in the executants to effect such alienation. 125. In the plaint, the plaintiffs assert that the property in Sy.No.85/1 measuring 3 acres 4 guntas belonged to the joint family and that the alienation of 1 acre 22 guntas in favour of the predecessors of the contesting defendants was made without legal necessity and without the consent of other co-parceners. It is contended that the said transaction was not for the benefit of the family and therefore cannot bind the plaintiffs' share. 126. The contesting defendants, in their written statements, have categorically denied these allegations. They have contended that the Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 was executed by competent members of the family including Thimmareddy, Venkataswamy Reddy and others, who were in lawful possession and enjoyment of the property. According to the defendants, the sale was validly executed and has been acted upon for several decades, 67 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment thereby vesting absolute title in the purchasers and their successors. 127. In order to prove their case, the plaintiffs examined Plaintiff No.3 as PW1. In her chief-examination, PW1 reiterated the allegations made in the plaint and stated that his father Thimmaiah and Defendant Nos.1 to 5 had no exclusive right to alienate the joint family property. However, the said assertion is largely a reiteration of the pleadings and is not supported by independent documentary evidence demonstrating the absence of authority or legal necessity. 128. During the course of cross-examination, PW1 admitted that the Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 had been executed by members of their family and that the said document was a registered instrument. He further admitted that neither he nor his predecessors had initiated any legal proceedings challenging the said alienation for several decades. Such admissions are significant as they 68 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment indicate that the transaction was not questioned during the lifetime of the alleged executants. 129. PW1 also admitted in cross-examination that he had not produced any document to show that the alienation was fraudulent, coercive, or unsupported by legal necessity. He conceded that he was not personally aware of the circumstances under which the sale deed was executed. These admissions substantially weaken the plaintiffs' attempt to invalidate the transaction. 130. On the contrary, the defendants examined DW1, who deposed that the property was purchased under a valid and registered Sale Deed executed in the year 1968. DW1 stated that the vendors were in lawful possession of the property at the time of the transaction and that the sale consideration was paid to them. He further deposed that the purchasers and their successors have been in continuous possession and enjoyment of the property ever since the purchase. 69 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 131. During cross-examination, nothing substantial was elicited from DW1 to discredit the execution or validity of the Sale Deed. Though suggestions were made alleging that the vendors had no authority to alienate the property, DW1 denied the same and maintained that the transaction was genuine and lawful. His testimony remains consistent and is supported by the documentary evidence placed on record. 132. The legal position governing alienation of joint family property is well settled. Under Hindu law, the Karta or adult co-parceners in management of the family property may alienate joint family property for legal necessity, for the benefit of the estate, or with the consent of other co-parceners. Once a registered alienation executed by competent family members is produced, the burden shifts upon the person challenging the transaction to establish that it was not supported by legal necessity or that the executants lacked authority. 70 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 133. In the present case, the plaintiffs have failed to discharge this burden. They have not produced any evidence demonstrating that the executants lacked authority or that the sale was tainted by illegality. The long lapse of time between the execution of the sale deed in 1968 and the institution of the present suit further strengthens the presumption that the transaction was genuine and was acted upon by the parties concerned. 134. The documentary records produced by the defendants, including mutation entries and revenue records, consistently reflect the names of the purchasers and their successors in respect of the alienated property. Such long-standing entries in public records lend considerable support to the defendants' case and reinforce the validity of the alienation. 135. The admissions made by PW1 in his cross- examination, coupled with the absence of documentary evidence from the plaintiffs' side and the consistent testimony of DW1, clearly indicate that the plaintiffs have 71 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment failed to establish that their father Thimmaiah and Defendant Nos.1 to 5 lacked authority to alienate the property. On the contrary, the material on record suggests that the alienation was executed by competent persons and has been acted upon for several decades. 136. In view of the foregoing discussion and appreciation of the oral and documentary evidence on record, and having regard to the settled legal principles governing alienation of joint family property, this Court is of the considered opinion that the plaintiffs have failed to prove that their father Thimmaiah and Defendant Nos.1 to 5 had no right, title, or authority to alienate the suit schedule property. Accordingly, Issue No.4 is answered in the Negative. 137. Issue No.5:- The burden of proving this issue squarely lies upon the plaintiffs. The plaintiffs have approached the Court seeking a declaration that the Sale Deeds dated 04.04.1968, 28.09.1981 and 11.05.2011 are not binding upon their alleged 4/9th share in the suit 72 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment schedule property. Such a declaration can be granted only if the plaintiffs establish that the executants of the said documents had no authority to alienate the property or that the alienation were otherwise invalid in law. 138. In the plaint, the plaintiffs have contended that the suit schedule property was the ancestral joint family property of the plaintiffs and Defendant Nos.1 to 5. It is their case that their father Thimmaiah and other members of the family had no exclusive right to alienate the property and that the Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 was executed without legal necessity and without the consent of the other co-parceners. The subsequent Sale Deeds dated 28.09.1981 and 11.05.2011 are alleged to be derivative transactions based upon the original alienation and therefore, according to the plaintiffs, are also not binding on their alleged share. 139. The contesting defendants, in their written statements, have denied these allegations and have contended that the Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 was 73 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment executed by competent members of the family who were in lawful possession of the property. It is further contended that the purchasers under the said sale deed obtained valid title and that the subsequent transactions in the years 1981 and 2011 are lawful transfers made by persons who had already acquired valid ownership. 140. In order to establish their claim, the plaintiffs examined Plaintiff No.1 as PW1. In his chief-examination affidavit, PW1 reiterated the averments of the plaint and stated that the sale deeds in question are not binding upon the plaintiffs' share. However, such statement is largely a reiteration of the pleadings and requires corroboration through cogent evidence demonstrating that the alienations were unauthorized or otherwise invalid. 141. During the cross-examination of PW1, it was elicited that the Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 is a registered document executed by members of the plaintiffs' family. PW1 admitted that he had not personally witnessed the execution of the document and that he had no direct 74 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment knowledge of the circumstances under which the sale was effected. He also admitted that neither he nor his predecessors had challenged the said alienation for several decades. 142. PW1 further admitted in cross-examination that the revenue records from the early 1970s onwards stand in the names of the purchasers and their successors. He also conceded that buildings have been constructed on the property and that the defendants and their predecessors have been paying taxes and exercising acts of ownership over the land. These admissions significantly weaken the plaintiffs' attempt to dispute the validity of the alienations. 143. PW1 was also confronted with the subsequent Sale Deeds dated 28.09.1981 and 11.05.2011. During cross-examination, he admitted that he had no personal knowledge regarding the execution of those transactions. He further admitted that he had not produced any documentary evidence to show that the vendors under those documents had no title to convey the property. 75 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 144. On the other hand, the defendants examined DW1, who deposed that the property measuring 1 acre 22 guntas in Sy.No.85/1 had been purchased under the registered Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968. DW1 further stated that the purchasers obtained valid title and were put in possession of the property. According to DW1, the subsequent Sale Deeds of 1981 and 2011 were executed by lawful owners and are valid transfers in accordance with law. 145. During cross-examination, DW1 consistently maintained that the property had been purchased through a valid transaction and that the defendants and their predecessors have been in continuous and open possession of the property ever since the purchase. Nothing material was elicited in cross-examination to discredit the execution or validity of the sale deeds relied upon by the defendants. 146. The documentary evidence produced by the defendants, including revenue records, mutation entries, and tax receipts, clearly indicate that the purchasers under 76 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment the Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 and their successors have been recognized as the owners in possession of the property for several decades. These records lend considerable support to the defendants' contention that the alienations were genuine and acted upon. 147. The legal position governing alienation of property by co-parceners under Hindu law is well settled. A Karta or managing member of a joint family may alienate joint family property for legal necessity or for the benefit of the estate. Once a registered sale deed executed by competent members is produced, a presumption arises in favour of its validity, and the burden shifts to the person challenging the transaction to prove that it was not supported by legal necessity or authority. 148. In the present case, the plaintiffs have not produced any evidence to establish that the Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 was executed without legal necessity or that the executants lacked authority. The long lapse of time between the execution of the said document and the 77 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment institution of the present suit further strengthens the presumption of validity attaching to a registered conveyance. 149. The subsequent Sale Deeds dated 28.09.1981 and 11.05.2011 are derivative transactions flowing from the title acquired under the original sale deed. When the original conveyance itself is not shown to be invalid, the subsequent transfers made by the lawful owners cannot be declared void or non-binding merely on the basis of the plaintiffs' assertions. 150. The admissions made by PW1 in cross- examination, coupled with the documentary evidence produced by the defendants and the consistent testimony of DW1, clearly indicate that the plaintiffs have failed to establish that the alienations in question are not binding on them. The evidence on record instead suggests that the purchasers and their successors have been in lawful possession and enjoyment of the property for a long period. 78 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 151. In view of the foregoing discussion, the Court is of the considered opinion that the plaintiffs have failed to prove that the Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968, the Sale Deed dated 28.09.1981, and the Sale Deed dated 11.05.2011 are not binding on their alleged 4/9th share in the suit schedule property. Accordingly, Issue No.5 is answered in the Negative. 152. Issue No.6:- The plaintiffs have sought the relief of permanent injunction restraining Defendant Nos.6 to 34 from interfering with their alleged possession and enjoyment of the suit schedule properties. The grant of a permanent injunction is governed by well-established principles of law. A plaintiff seeking such relief must prove lawful possession over the property on the date of the suit and establish that the defendants have either interfered with or threatened to interfere with such possession. 153. In the plaint, the plaintiffs have contended that the suit schedule properties form part of the joint family estate and that the plaintiffs along with Defendant Nos.1 to 79 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 5 are in joint possession and enjoyment thereof. It is further alleged that Defendant Nos.6 to 34, claiming under certain sale transactions, have attempted to interfere with the plaintiffs' peaceful possession, thereby compelling them to seek the relief of permanent injunction. 154. The contesting defendants, particularly Defendant Nos.6 to 34, have strongly denied these allegations in their written statements. It is their specific contention that the plaintiffs have never been in possession of the suit schedule properties and that the defendants have derived valid title through successive sale deeds originating from the registered Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968. According to them, the purchasers and their successors have been in continuous and exclusive possession of the property for several decades. 155. In order to substantiate their case, the plaintiffs examined Plaintiff No.1 as PW1. In her chief-examination, PW1 reiterated the allegations made in the plaint and asserted that the plaintiffs are in joint possession of the 80 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment suit schedule properties. He further deposed that the defendants attempted to interfere with their possession, thereby necessitating the filing of the suit. 156. However, during the course of cross- examination, PW1 admitted that she has not produced any documentary evidence such as revenue records, RTC extracts, mutation entries, or tax receipts to demonstrate that the plaintiffs are in possession of the suit schedule properties. She also admitted that he has not cultivated the land nor exercised any acts of ownership over the property in recent decades. 157. PW1 further admitted in cross-examination that the revenue records stand in the names of the purchasers and their successors for many years. She also conceded that the defendants and their predecessors have constructed buildings and are paying property taxes. These admissions clearly indicate that the plaintiffs are not in actual possession of the property. 81 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 158. On the other hand, the defendants examined DW1, who deposed that the property was lawfully purchased under the registered Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 and that the purchasers and their successors have been in open, continuous, and exclusive possession of the property ever since. DW1 also stated that the defendants have constructed buildings, obtained municipal records, and have been paying taxes regularly. 159. During cross-examination, nothing material was elicited from DW1 to discredit his testimony regarding possession. Though suggestions were made disputing the defendants' possession, DW1 consistently maintained that the plaintiffs have never been in possession and that the defendants alone have been enjoying the property as owners. 160. The documentary evidence produced by the defendants, including revenue records and tax receipts, corroborates the testimony of DW1 and clearly demonstrates that the defendants and their predecessors 82 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment have been exercising acts of ownership over the property for a considerable length of time. 161. It is a settled principle of law that a decree for permanent injunction cannot be granted in favour of a person who is not in possession of the property. The relief of injunction is intended to protect existing possession and cannot be used as a means to recover possession from another party who is in settled occupation of the property. 162. The Supreme Court and various High Courts have consistently held that when the plaintiff is not in possession and the title itself is disputed, the appropriate remedy is to seek declaration and recovery of possession rather than a bare injunction. In the present case, the evidence clearly indicates that the plaintiffs are not in possession of the suit schedule property. 163. Furthermore, the findings recorded under the earlier issues indicate that the plaintiffs have failed to establish that the suit schedule property continues to be joint family property or that the alienations relied upon by 83 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment the defendants are not binding. In the absence of proof of title and possession, the plaintiffs cannot seek the equitable relief of injunction. 164. The admissions made by PW1 during cross- examination, coupled with the documentary evidence produced by the defendants and the consistent testimony of DW1, clearly demonstrate that the defendants are in settled and exclusive possession of the property. Consequently, the plaintiffs cannot claim that their possession has been interfered with by the defendants. 165. When the defendants themselves are found to be in lawful possession and enjoyment of the property, the question of restraining them by way of permanent injunction does not arise. Granting such relief in favour of the plaintiffs would amount to protecting a non-existent possession and disturbing the settled possession of the defendants. 166. In view of the foregoing discussion and appreciation of the oral and documentary evidence on 84 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment record, as well as the settled principles governing the grant of permanent injunction, this Court is of the considered opinion that the plaintiffs are not entitled to the relief of permanent injunction against Defendant Nos.6 to 34. Accordingly, Issue No.6 is answered in the Negative. 167. Issue No.7:- The plaintiffs have sought the relief of partition and separate possession of their alleged 4/9th share in the suit schedule properties, along with consequential relief of permanent injunction against the defendants. The entitlement to such relief depends upon the successful establishment of the foundational facts pleaded in the plaint. The burden lies upon the plaintiffs to prove that the suit schedule properties are joint family properties and that they continue to have a subsisting right and possession therein. 168. As discussed while answering the earlier issues, the plaintiffs have failed to establish that they and Defendant Nos.1 to 5 constitute members of a Hindu undivided joint family in respect of the suit schedule 85 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment properties. The oral testimony of PW1, particularly in his cross-examination, reveals that he has not produced any documentary evidence to show that the property continued to be part of the joint family estate after the execution of the registered Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968. On the contrary, the evidence placed by the defendants indicates that the property had already been alienated and that the purchasers and their successors have been in possession. 169. The evidence of DW1, supported by documentary records such as revenue entries and tax receipts, clearly demonstrates that the defendants and their predecessors have been in continuous and exclusive possession of the property for several decades. Nothing substantial has been elicited in the cross-examination of DW1 to discredit his testimony. The admissions made by PW1 during cross-examination further weaken the plaintiffs' claim regarding joint possession and the invalidity of the alienations. 86 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 170. It is a settled principle of law that the relief of partition and consequential injunction can be granted only when the plaintiff establishes a clear legal right over the property and proves that such right has been infringed. In the present case, the plaintiffs have failed to establish the joint family nature of the property, the invalidity of the sale transactions, or their possession over the suit schedule properties. Consequently, the plaintiffs have not succeeded in proving the essential ingredients required for grant of the relief sought. 171. In view of the findings recorded on the preceding issues and upon appreciation of the pleadings and the oral and documentary evidence on record, this Court is of the considered opinion that the plaintiffs are not entitled to the reliefs as prayed for in the plaint. Accordingly, Issue No.7 is answered in the Negative. 172. Additional Issue Nos.1 to 3:- These additional issues arise from the specific defence raised by Defendant No.15 in his written statement. Defendant No.15 contends 87 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment that he has purchased a residential revenue site formed in Item No.2 of the suit schedule property under a registered Sale Deed dated 25.10.2000 and that the said purchase was made from persons who had derived valid title through earlier transactions. He further contends that the alienations made by Defendant Nos.1 to 5 were for the necessity and benefit of the joint family and therefore bind the plaintiffs. 173. In the written statement filed by Defendant No.15, it is specifically pleaded that the vendors who executed the sale deed in his favour had derived valid title through a chain of registered transactions commencing from the original Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968. It is further pleaded that the property was subsequently converted into revenue sites and several purchasers including Defendant No.15 acquired different portions through registered documents. 174. The plaintiffs, in their plaint as well as during the course of trial, have disputed the validity of these 88 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment alienations. According to the plaintiffs, the property was joint family property and the alienations made by Defendant Nos.1 to 5 were not supported by legal necessity and therefore are not binding upon their alleged share. 175. In order to substantiate their case, the plaintiffs examined Plaintiff No.3 as PW1. In her chief-examination affidavit, PW1 reiterated the pleadings of the plaint and asserted that the alienations made in respect of the suit schedule properties were not binding upon the plaintiffs. He further stated that the defendants have formed sites and sold them to various purchasers without authority. 176. However, during the cross-examination of PW1, it was elicited that the original Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 was a registered document executed by members of the plaintiffs' family. PW1 admitted that she had no personal knowledge regarding the circumstances under which the sale was executed and that she had not produced any document showing that the said alienation was without legal necessity. 89 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 177. PW1 further admitted that after the execution of the Sale Deed of 1968, several transactions have taken place in respect of the property and that different purchasers have acquired sites formed in the land. She also admitted that she has not challenged those transactions for several decades. 178. PW1 also conceded during cross-examination that the revenue records and other documents stand in the names of the purchasers and their successors. She further admitted that houses have been constructed on several sites and that the occupants are residing there for many years. These admissions clearly indicate that the property has undergone substantial change in character and has been developed into residential sites. 179. On the other hand, the defendants examined DW1 who supported the defence taken in the written statements. DW1 deposed that the land was validly sold under the registered Sale Deed dated 04.04.1968 and that 90 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment thereafter the purchasers formed residential sites and sold them to various persons including Defendant No.15. 180. DW1 further deposed that Defendant No.15 purchased a residential revenue site under the registered Sale Deed dated 25.10.2000 bearing Document No.6071/1999-2000 and that the said transaction was effected after verifying the title of the vendors. According to DW1, the purchaser was put in possession of the site immediately after the execution of the sale deed. 181. During the cross-examination of DW1, nothing substantial was elicited to discredit his testimony regarding the sale transactions or the possession of Defendant No.15. Though suggestions were made disputing the validity of the transactions, DW1 denied the same and maintained that the purchasers including Defendant No.15 acquired valid title. 182. The registered Sale Deed dated 25.10.2000 relied upon by Defendant No.15 is a document of title which carries a presumption of genuineness under law. 91 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment The plaintiffs have not produced any evidence to show that the said document was obtained by fraud, misrepresentation, or that the vendors had no authority to convey the property. 183. It is a well-settled principle of law that a purchaser who acquires property for valuable consideration under a registered sale deed after making due enquiries regarding the title of the vendor is regarded as a bona-fide purchaser for value. Such purchaser is entitled to protection, particularly when the transaction has been acted upon and possession has been delivered. 184. In the present case, Defendant No.15 has produced the registered sale deed in his favour and has asserted that he purchased the site after verifying the title of his vendors. The plaintiffs have not produced any evidence to show that Defendant No.15 had knowledge of any alleged defect in title. 185. The evidence on record further indicates that the site purchased by Defendant No.15 forms part of a 92 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment layout where several residential houses have been constructed. Such development of the property and the existence of several independent purchasers further support the case of Defendant No.15 that he purchased the site in good faith. 186. With regard to possession, the evidence of DW1 indicates that Defendant No.15 has been in possession and enjoyment of the residential site from the date of purchase. The plaintiffs have not produced any documentary evidence to show that they were ever in possession of the said site or that Defendant No.15 was dispossessed. 187. PW1, during his cross-examination, admitted that she has not produced any document showing that she exercised acts of possession over the sites formed in Item No.2 of the suit schedule property. She also admitted that several purchasers have been residing on the sites formed in the property. 188. The long and uninterrupted possession of purchasers including Defendant No.15 also finds support 93 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment from the surrounding circumstances such as construction of houses, payment of taxes, and the absence of any earlier challenge by the plaintiffs. Such possession carries considerable evidentiary value. 189. The legal position governing alienation of joint family property also assumes significance. Even assuming that the property originally belonged to the joint family, alienations made by the Karta or managing members for legal necessity or for the benefit of the estate bind the other co-parceners. The burden lies upon the person challenging the transaction to prove absence of such necessity. 190. In the present case, the plaintiffs have failed to discharge that burden. They have not produced any evidence to demonstrate that the alienations made by Defendant Nos.1 to 5 were without authority or necessity. On the contrary, the chain of transactions and the long lapse of time support the presumption that the alienations were genuine and acted upon. 94 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment 191. In view of the above discussion and appreciation of the oral and documentary evidence on record, this Court is of the considered opinion that Defendant No.15 has successfully established that he is a bona-fide purchaser of the residential revenue site under the registered Sale Deed dated 25.10.2000 and that he is in possession and enjoyment of the said property. The evidence also supports the defence that the alienations made by Defendant Nos.1 to 5 were acted upon and form the basis of subsequent transactions. Accordingly, the above additional issues No.1 to 3 are answered in the Affirmative. 192. Issue No.8:- In view of the findings on Issues No.1 to 7 and Addl. Issue No.1 to 3, I proceed to pass the following: ORDER The suit filed by the Plaintiffs against the defendants seeking for partition, declaration and consequential relief of permanent injunction, is hereby dismissed. 95 O. S.69 /2012 Judgment No order as to costs. Draw decree in the matter accordingly.
